name: glaive of invocation
shortDescription: A ritual glaive with a long hooked blade and a shaft marked for summoning rites rather than ordinary soldiering. Its balance suggests ceremony as much as combat, and collectors pay well when the etched calls along the haft still catch candlelight.
flavorText: “Some weapons are swung. This one is asked.”

name: scepter of invocation
shortDescription: A formal scepter used to begin calls, rites, bargains, and other matters that should not start accidentally. Its headpiece is shaped to draw attention, making it valuable to court mages, temple speakers, and anyone paid to make silence gather in a room.
flavorText: “Raise it first, speak second, regret third.”

name: scarce scepter of power
shortDescription: A scarce scepter heavy with gold fittings, old enchantment marks, and the uncomfortable confidence of an object made for command. Buyers inspect it carefully, because true power leaves traces in the metal while false power usually leaves only polish.
flavorText: “Authority is louder when it has a handle.”

name: fabled scepter of reality
shortDescription: A fabled scepter said to steady illusions, expose false glamour, and make uncertain rooms feel painfully solid. Whether the stories are true or not, scholars and rulers bid high for any relic rumored to make the world obey plain fact.
flavorText: “Most magic bends the world. This one is accused of straightening it.”

name: scepter of torment
shortDescription: A dark scepter with harsh ornament and a reputation that lowers offers from decent buyers while raising them from secretive ones. It is traded carefully, wrapped well, and never displayed too cheerfully, since even rumors can stain a respectable stall.
flavorText: “Some relics do not curse the owner. They curse the conversation.”

name: scepter of burial
shortDescription: A grave-rite scepter carved with funerary symbols and used by priests, wardens, and tomb keepers during solemn work. Its value rises in old cities where families care deeply about correct ceremony and the dead are expected to stay respectfully accounted for.
flavorText: “A proper burial has tools, witnesses, and no applause.”

name: fabled scepter of ultimate power
shortDescription: A fabled scepter with a title large enough to make every appraiser skeptical and every noble interested. Its metalwork is magnificent, its stories contradictory, and its price often depends on whether the buyer wants power or simply wants others to think so.
flavorText: “Ultimate power is usually listed above market value.”

name: scarce sceptre of bones
shortDescription: A scarce sceptre built with pale bone motifs and old sepulcher craft, unsettling in the hand but beautifully made. Necromancers, collectors, and dramatic nobles all ask after it, though only the collectors admit the decoration is the main appeal.
flavorText: “Bone remembers shape long after pride forgets its owner.”

name: fabled scepter of the sea
shortDescription: A fabled sea scepter set with blue-green stones and salt-worn symbols of tide, storm, and drowned kingdoms. It commands high prices in harbors, where sailors are practical enough to mock legends only after the relic has safely left the room.
flavorText: “The sea does not bow, but it enjoys being negotiated with.”

name: fabled scepter of legacy
shortDescription: A fabled scepter passed through too many claimed bloodlines for any ledger to remain calm. Dynasties, heirs, and counterfeit heirs all value it because legacy is often less about proof than about holding the right object while speaking confidently.
flavorText: “Inheritance becomes simpler when everyone points at the same relic.”

name: scarce scepter of heritage
shortDescription: A scarce ceremonial scepter marked with family patterns, old seals, and regional metalwork that suggests careful lineage. It appeals to archivists, noble houses, and ambitious merchants purchasing ancestry one impressive artifact at a time.
flavorText: “Heritage is history after it has been polished.”

name: scarce scepter of decomposition
shortDescription: A scarce scepter with greened metal, cracked enamel, and charms associated with rot, compost, and patient endings. Farmers' cults, swamp apothecaries, and darker scholars value it, though cautious merchants keep it far from fresh produce displays.
flavorText: “Decay has its own priesthood and very particular storage needs.”

name: scepter of flight
shortDescription: A light scepter feathered with airy ornament and balanced as if it dislikes remaining still. Falconers, wind mages, and wealthy travelers ask about it often, hoping the enchantment is useful and not merely a poetic way to drop valuables from heights.
flavorText: “Anything promising flight should be sold with a return policy.”

name: market scepter of stone
shortDescription: A market-grade scepter set with plain stone, sturdy enough for ritual display and cheap enough for traveling performers or village shrines. It lacks rare enchantment, but its weight gives ceremonies a satisfying seriousness that painted wood cannot manage.
flavorText: “Stone is the affordable cousin of eternity.”

name: scarce scepter of judgement
shortDescription: A scarce scepter used in tribunals, oath halls, and stern rituals where decisions must look heavier than opinion. Its straight lines and cool metal make it popular with magistrates who understand that justice benefits from good staging.
flavorText: “A verdict lands harder when the table already fears the symbol.”

name: market scepter of wood
shortDescription: A simple wooden scepter for village rites, theater troupes, classrooms, and small temples that need a symbol without bankrupting the box. It is light, repairable, and honest about being ceremonial rather than miraculous.
flavorText: “Wooden authority is still authority if everyone agrees to pretend.”

name: fabled scepter of Unraveling
shortDescription: A fabled scepter associated with undone knots, broken spells, and plans that collapse neatly from the center. It is purchased cautiously by mages and even more cautiously by merchants, since anything called Unraveling makes contracts feel nervous.
flavorText: “Some relics solve problems by pulling the wrong thread first.”

name: scepter of Long Dusk
shortDescription: A shadow-toned scepter named for the hour when roads empty and candles become important. It appeals to twilight orders, night watch captains, and collectors of melancholy magic, especially when its dark stones seem to hold the last light of evening.
flavorText: “Dusk is patient. It wins every day.”

name: scepter of growth
shortDescription: A living-green scepter wrapped in leafwork and favored by hedge mages, orchard priests, and hopeful landowners. Buyers expect blessings for crops and gardens, though experienced merchants promise only that the carvings are fertile and the price negotiable.
flavorText: “Growth magic is popular because everyone owns something that refuses to improve.”

name: scepter of blood
shortDescription: A crimson-marked scepter used in kinship rites, oath bargains, and older ceremonies best described politely. Its value comes from intensity and taboo, attracting collectors who claim scholarly interest while asking far too many practical questions.
flavorText: “Blood makes promises that ink is too timid to attempt.”

name: scarce scepter of sight
shortDescription: A scarce scepter set with eye motifs and clear stones, said to sharpen awareness and reveal what careless people miss. Scouts, judges, and paranoid nobles all want it, though the best merchants remind them that seeing clearly is not the same as understanding.
flavorText: “More sight has ruined more lies than swords ever did.”

name: scarce scepter of vision
shortDescription: A scarce scepter associated with dreams, prophecy, and expensive guesses about tomorrow. Seers prize it for ritual focus, rulers prize it for confidence, and merchants prize it because the future is always easier to sell before it arrives.
flavorText: “Prophecy is forecast, theater, and invoice in one breath.”

name: market scepter of celebration
shortDescription: A bright market scepter made for festivals, weddings, harvest rites, and town pageants where cheer matters more than power. It is sturdy enough for processions and flashy enough for children to point at, which often decides the sale.
flavorText: “Not every ritual needs thunder; some need ribbons.”

name: scepter of the mummy
shortDescription: A desert-styled scepter wrapped in linen bands, old gold, and tomb motifs that smell faintly of dust even when clean. Antiquarians and curse-hunters ask for it by reputation, while careful shopkeepers keep it away from nervous customers.
flavorText: “Ancient things sell best when they look like they might object.”

name: fabled scepter of Gravebinding
shortDescription: A fabled funerary scepter linked to warded tombs, sealed crypts, and old laws between the living and the buried. Its craftsmanship is exceptional, but its market is narrow, consisting mostly of priests, scholars, and buyers who speak too softly.
flavorText: “A grave properly bound becomes a door no one admits seeing.”

name: scepter of brilliance
shortDescription: A radiant scepter with bright stones and polished metalwork that catches light from every flattering angle. Court mages, celebrants, and vain patrons enjoy it because brilliance is useful magic and also an excellent excuse for being noticed.
flavorText: “Shine first. Explain later.”

name: scepter of illusion
shortDescription: A scepter made for glamour work, false images, and the delicate art of making audiences trust what they want to see. Its surface seems to shift under lamplight, which helps both the enchantment and the asking price.
flavorText: “Illusion is cheaper than truth and often better dressed.”

name: scarce scepter of magna
shortDescription: A scarce scepter bearing old magna marks whose exact meaning depends on which scholar is currently being paid. The relic has weight, polish, and enough uncertainty to attract collectors who enjoy owning mysteries with impressive handles.
flavorText: “Dead languages add value when nobody can correct the label.”

name: fabled scepter of the octopus king
shortDescription: A fabled scepter crowned with curling tentacle forms, pearl-dark stones, and maritime royal symbolism from stranger courts. It fascinates harbor collectors and deeply unsettles land nobles, which makes it excellent stock for merchants with theatrical display cases.
flavorText: “Eight arms make for complicated succession.”

name: scarce scepter of winter
shortDescription: A scarce winter scepter of pale metal and cold-blue ornament, cool to the touch even in warm rooms. Northern houses, frost mages, and collectors of seasonal rites value it, though no one likes paying extra for packing straw that freezes overnight.
flavorText: “Winter always arrives with confidence and terrible timing.”

name: scarce wand of bones
shortDescription: A scarce wand carved with bone-white fittings and thin grave-charms, lighter than the matching scepters but no less unsettling. It is favored for precise rites, quiet work, and collectors who prefer their ominous purchases easy to hide in a sleeve.
flavorText: “A wand whispers what a staff would shout.”

name: wand of hatred
shortDescription: A bitter little wand with dark grain, sharp angles, and a reputation for focusing grudges into troublesome magic. Few respectable shops display it openly, but there is always someone willing to pay for a tool that makes resentment feel productive.
flavorText: “Hatred is a poor fuel, but it burns easily.”

name: fabled wand of Gravebinding
shortDescription: A fabled wand used in delicate tomb wards, burial seals, and the closing of places that should remain closed. Its small size makes it more practical than ceremonial, which only increases demand among specialists who dislike carrying obvious relics through town.
flavorText: “The smallest lock may guard the oldest door.”

name: wand of action
shortDescription: A quick wand made for motion, impulse, and spellwork that prefers doing over deliberating. Adventurers like its directness, teachers dislike its impatience, and merchants enjoy how often buyers return asking whether there are calmer versions.
flavorText: “It rarely waits for the second half of an instruction.”

name: rough wand of infection
shortDescription: A rough wand stained by unpleasant herbal seals and folk charms associated with sickness, spreading rot, or warding it away depending on who tells the story. It is cheap for a magical implement, but never sold beside food or perfume.
flavorText: “Some tools are wrapped twice and explained once.”

name: wand of water
shortDescription: A blue-touched wand suited to small water charms, rain blessings, cleansing rites, and sailors who want a little control over an element famous for ignoring requests. It sells steadily in ports, farms, and any town with unreliable wells.
flavorText: “Water obeys politely right up to the moment it does not.”

name: wand of focus
shortDescription: A plain, well-balanced wand used to steady attention and guide precise spellwork without unnecessary sparkle. Students, scribes, and tired mages value it because concentration is one of the few magical ingredients that cannot be bought in bulk.
flavorText: “A focused wand does not make the spell easier; it makes excuses harder.”

name: wand of pollution
shortDescription: A stained wand tied to sludge charms, spoiled waters, smoke rites, and the study of how clean things become costly problems. It attracts alchemists and inspectors more than heroes, but those markets pay reliably when trouble needs naming.
flavorText: “Dirt becomes valuable the moment someone needs proof.”

name: fabled wand of Reshaping
shortDescription: A fabled wand associated with altered forms, repaired mistakes, and experiments that begin with confidence and end with new terminology. Artificers and ambitious mages prize it highly, while cautious merchants ask whether the crate was always that shape.
flavorText: “Reshaping is transformation with fewer apologies.”

name: scarce wand of lies
shortDescription: A scarce wand used in glamour, misdirection, and the kind of social magic that makes honest people count their rings afterward. It is valuable, dangerous to advertise, and usually sold to buyers who insist they only need it for research.
flavorText: “A lie with a wand feels almost official.”

name: fabled wand of Unraveling
shortDescription: A fabled wand for undoing knots of spellwork, fabric, memory, or luck, depending on which legend is currently profitable. Its power is said to be precise, but merchants still keep it far from ledgers, stitches, and complicated promises.
flavorText: “Give it a loose thread and it will ask for the whole coat.”

name: gold gem wand
shortDescription: A gold-set wand capped with a bright gem, showy enough for courts and expensive enough for apprentices to stare at from across the shop. It may be practical, but its first magic is always making the buyer feel richer while holding it.
flavorText: “Gold improves many spells, especially persuasion.”

name: fabled wand of Lantern-Summoning
shortDescription: A fabled wand known for calling small lights, guide flames, and lantern spirits in dark places. Travelers, miners, and temple wardens adore the stories, while merchants adore a relic that sells safety, wonder, and replacement candles in the same breath.
flavorText: “Darkness hates a reliable supplier.”

name: wand of blood
shortDescription: A crimson wand used in oath craft, lineage charms, battlefield medicine, and older rites that respectable mages describe very carefully. Its demand is steady but private, since buyers rarely want their household staff asking why the wand has that color.
flavorText: “Some magic checks identity at the pulse.”

name: fabled wand of Illusion
shortDescription: A fabled wand of Illusion with shifting lacquer and a gem that never looks quite the same twice. Performers, spies, and court magicians bid fiercely for it, though the wisest merchants demand payment before demonstrating anything uncertain.
flavorText: “The finest illusion is convincing the buyer the price was their idea.”

name: wand of passion
shortDescription: A warm-toned wand linked to inspiration, desire, zeal, and the sort of emotional weather that makes sensible plans catch fire. Artists, duelists, poets, and reckless nobles all ask for it, usually with more confidence than caution.
flavorText: “Passion gives terrible advice in a magnificent voice.”

name: scarce wand of weight
shortDescription: A scarce wand used for heaviness charms, burden tricks, and the satisfying correction of objects that refuse to stay put. Dock mages, builders, and petty pranksters all value it, though only the builders admit the practical applications first.
flavorText: “Gravity is common. Controlling it is not.”

name: wand of sound
shortDescription: A wand tuned for chimes, echoes, projected voices, and disturbances that can be blamed on acoustics if the guard arrives. Bards, heralds, temple callers, and nuisance-makers all find uses for it, making its market louder than average.
flavorText: “Every spell is improved by entering dramatically.”

name: fabled wand of enchanting
shortDescription: A fabled wand made for laying charm into objects, strengthening lesser magic, and giving ordinary goods reasons to cost more. Enchanters treat it as a serious tool; merchants treat it as proof that accessories can be an investment.
flavorText: “Enchanting is craftsmanship with better rumors.”

name: scarce wand of seeing
shortDescription: A scarce wand of seeing used to sharpen perception, reveal hidden marks, and make suspicious details harder to ignore. Appraisers, scouts, investigators, and nervous nobles all want one, though none enjoy discovering exactly what they paid not to miss.
flavorText: “Seeing more is useful until the invoice becomes clearer.”
